subject: Re: Export Subtomograms STAR file format
body:
Hi again,

I tried again and ran into a few problems, I've detailed the process I went 
through below:


   - Loaded frames folder in warp in tomostar mode (all tilt series show up)
   - Click export subtomograms
   - Select STAR file (table_cleaned.star attached)
   - set options relating to pixel size etc
   - Press export
   - Export starts! It seems to have found the first tilt-series, I can 
   hear the hard drive whirring away, frames/subtomo/ts*.mrc get created and 
   files are being written out, subtomograms and CTF volumes.
   - These volumes are all black, both the CTF and the subtomogram
   - GPU memory was full during extraction
   - After a while, the program crashes with the following error

System.Exception: cudaErrorIllegalAddress
   at Warp.GPU.CheckGPUExceptions() in D:\Dev\warp2\WarpLib\GPU.cs:line 1151
   at 
Warp.TiltSeries.ReconstructSubtomos(ProcessingOptionsTomoSubReconstruction 
options, float3[] positions, float3[] angles) in 
D:\Dev\warp2\WarpLib\TiltSeries.cs:line 2957
   at WarpWorker.Program.Main(String[] args) in 
D:\Dev\warp2\WarpWorker\Program.cs:line 223


Can you see anything obviously wrong with the starfile or is this likely to 
be a GPU memory problem? 

Thanks in advance,

Alister

On Friday, 31 January 2020 20:00:04 UTC+1, Dimitry Tegunov wrote:
>
> Hi Alister,
>
> that's the rlnMicrographName column, like with 2D data.
>
> Cheers,
> Dimitry
>
>
> On Friday, January 31, 2020 at 11:17:31 AM UTC+1, Alister Burt wrote:
>>
>> Hi all,
>>
>> I'm looking to extract subtomograms from a single star file in warp, 
>> following this section of the user guide
>>
>> Before you see the actual dialog, Warp will ask you to provide a file 
>>> with particle coordinates. Only RELION’s STAR format is currently 
>>> supported. The file must contain at least the rlnCoordinateX/Y/Z columns. 
>>> If rlnOriginX/Y/Z are specified, Warp will consider them, and the values 
>>> will be reset to 0 in the STAR file Warp generates. You can use either a 
>>> single file for all tilt series or, if you have one file per tilt series 
>>> (e. g. after template matching) with names formatted as [series 
>>> name]_suffix.star, you can point Warp to one of those files and it will 
>>> figure out the suffix automatically and find all remaining files in the 
>>> same folder. In the second case, *One input file per tilt series* will 
>>> become checked. If you’d like to use just the file you selected and 
>>> reconstruct sub-tomograms from only one tilt series, you can uncheck it.
>>>
>>
>> I'm just not sure exactly how to set up my star file so that warp knows 
>> which tilt series to reconstruct the particles from, what's the column name 
>> necessary?
>>
>> Thanks in advance,
>>
>> Alister
>>
>
